rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT DISPOSITION: $1,540.00 U.S. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 3, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S MOTION TO STRIKE. The People's motion to strike the claim of Trevor Merz is denied. Mr. Merz timely filed a claim opposing forfeiture bearing an electronic verification. Even if that verification was technically deficient, the failure to verify a pleading-even where the verification is required by statute-is a mere defect curable by amendment, even when submitted after the statute of limitations has run on the time to file the pleading. (United Farm Workers of America v. ALRB (1984) 37 Cal.3d 912, 915; Board of Trustees v. Superior Court (2007) 149 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1163-64.) In accordance with California's strong policies favoring liberal amendment of pleadings and resolution of disputes on their merits, Mr. Merz's claim opposing forfeiture is deemed timely filed on November 9, 2018. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Claimant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PETER MCDONALD ET AL VS. 830 LAKE STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANTS 830 LAKE STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION, MATTHIAS SPANLE, MARK SHER, PAMELA YOON'S Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement. Defendants 830 Lake Street Homeowners' Association, Matthias Spanle, Mark Sher, and Pamela Yoon's motion to enforce settlement agreement is granted and the court orders this case dismissed with prejudice pursuant to 2.2 of the parties' settlement agreement. The declaration of Frederick Seher establishes that he conducted a "survey" of the garage within the meaning of California Business & Professions Code § 8726 and that survey comports with 9 of the settlement agreement. Therefore, dismissal is warranted. The court awards $950 to defendants as reasonable fees and costs for bringing the instant motion. (Settlement, 8.2; Tursi Dec., 10.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY JOY HOM VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash And Modify Subpoenas Issued By Defendant San Francisco Zoological Society, And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF KIMBERLY HOM's Motion To Quash And Modify Subpoenas Issued By Defendant San Francisco Zoological Society, And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted in part; denied in part. The subpoenas to the medical providers shall be limited to 10 years prior to the incident. The records shall be first produced to Plaintiff's counsel for a "first look." If Plaintiff's counsel believes that any records are irrelevant and should not be produced, they are directed to meet and confer with Defendant's counsel to attempt to agree to a narrower scope. If an agreement can't be reached, Plaintiff's counsel shall submit the records to JPT McDonald for an in-camera review. All requests for sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY JOY HOM VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Medical Examination Of Plaintiff By Defendant San Francisco Zoological Society
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO ZOOLOGICAL SOCIETY's Motion To Compel Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel a further physical examination is granted. Dr. Strassberg shall focus primarily on conducting the physical examination but may ask questions that will allow him to form an intelligent opinion of Plaintiff's injuries. See Sharaff v. Superior Court (1955) 22 Cal.2d 508. The Court does not believe that a "full medical history" is necessary for Dr. Strassberg to render an opinion but he shall be allowed to ask questions about Plaintiff's past medical conditions if they directly relate to the conditions at issue in the litigation. The conditions at issue are those listed in Exh. M to the Garrett declaration filed in support of this motion. Plaintiff's counsel reserves the right to object to Dr. Strassberg's questions but shall not terminate the examination again unless the questioning is truly oppressive. All requests for sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN T BUI VS. JAMES E. ROBERTS-OBAYASHI CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Orders For Compelling Production Of Documents At Deposition And Further Deposition Testimony From Defendant Marcos Alvarez Casteneda Regarding Same
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF JOHN BUI's Motion For Orders For Compelling Production Of Documents At Deposition And Further Deposition Testimony From Defendant Marcos Alvarez Casteneda Regarding Same Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted in part. The Court finds that the records of Mr. Casteneda's cell phone calls on the day of the incident are relevant, however, Defendants need only produce documents that are in their possession, custody, and control. The Court finds that the declaration of Brendan Radich is insufficient since it does not detail his efforts to obtain the cell phone records from AT&T and it appears that AT&T might be able to locate records beyond two years. Also, there needs to be a declaration from Mr. Casteneda attesting that he has searched for and attempted to locate all records for any cell phones (personal or company) that he had in his possession on the day of the incident. Defendants shall produce any documents they locate, or the declarations referenced herein, no later than February 13, 2018. The requests for sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES H DELACEY VS. RAFAEL ALVAREZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT RAPHAEL ALVAREZ'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. Defendant Raphael Alvarez's motion to quash service of the summons and complaint, to strike the complaint, and to dismiss the case on the grounds of lack of jurisdiction and inconvenient forum is denied. Mr. Alvarez did not file a motion to quash service of summons on the ground of lack of jurisdiction of the court over him or a motion to stay or dismiss the action on the ground of inconvenient forum within the time allowed for filing a response to the complaint, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 418.10(a). Instead, on November 9, 2018, he filed an answer to the complaint. He did not file the instant motion until December 21, 2018. Having failed to file a timely motion to quash, stay, or dismiss simultaneously with his answer, as allowed by Code of Civil Procedure section 418.10(e), defendant waived any objection to the court's exercise of personal jurisdiction by making a general appearance in the action. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1014 ["A defendant appears in an action when the defendant answers . . . ."]; Roy v. Superior Court (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 337, 342.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 7, Defendant Hanson Bridgett LLP'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. The RFPD supplemental responses shall not give any qualifiers or statements other than the following: "Plaintiffs will produced all responsive, non-privileged documents in their possession, custody or control." The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NANCY ROMNEY VS. EDWARDS LIFESCIENCES, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Edwards Lifesciences, Llc, Edwards Lifesciences Corporation, Edwards Lifesciences(U.S.)Inc.'S Demurrer To Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 13, Defendants Edwards Lifesciences, Llc, Edwards Lifesciences Corporation, Edwards Lifesciences(U.S.)Inc.'S Demurrer To Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) Plaintiff's third cause of action for failure to warn is also defective in its current form, in that it fails to address the FDA's Instructions for Use for the Sapien 3, of which the Court takes judicial notice. The Instructions for Use specifically warn of the risk of rupture of the delivery system in the catheter's balloon and the possibility that this can lead to serious cardiovascular injury or even death. To the extent Plaintiff contends that Defendants should have provided warnings beyond those approved and mandated by the FDA, her claim is expressly preempted. (Riegel, 552 U.S. at 322-23; Coleman v. Medtronic, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 413, 427 [failure to warn claim preempted to extent it was based on theory that Medtronic should have given different warnings than those approved by FDA].) Plaintiff has leave to amend this cause of action if she can in good faith allege a viable failure to warn claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NANCY ROMNEY VS. EDWARDS LIFESCIENCES, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANTS EDWARDS LIFESCIENCES, LLC, EDWARDS LIFESCIENCES CORPORATION, EDWARDS LIFESCIENCES(U.S.)INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (Part 1 of 2) Defendants Edwards Lifesciences, LLC, Edwards Lifesciences Corp., and Edwards Lifesciences (U.S.) Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Nancy Romney's complaint is sustained as to the first cause of action for strict liability manufacturing defect, the second cause of action for negligence in manufacturing, and the third cause of action for failure to warn, with 30 days leave to amend. As pled, the manufacturing defect and negligent manufacturing claims are conclusory and do not allege sufficient ultimate facts to provide support for the assertion that Defendants violated federal law. The court need not accept as true the conclusions of law in the complaint. (Evans v. City of Berkeley (2006) 38 Cal.4th 1, 6; see also Evraets v. Intermedics Introcular, Inc. (1994) 29 Cal.App.4th 779, 794 [holding that pleading was inadequate because it failed to "illuminate which of the innumerable regulatory requirements respondents failed to satisfy"].) If these claims are to avoid dismissal, Plaintiff must amend the complaint to add allegations sufficient to overcome both the express and implied preemption provisions of the Medical Device Amendments (MDA) to the federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FDCA), 21 U.S.C. §§ 337(a), 360k(a). (See Riegel v. Medtronic, Inc. (2008) 552 U.S. 312, 321-330 [strict liability, breach of implied warranty, and negligence claims relating to balloon catheter used in angioplasty preempted under MPA]; see also Glennen v. Allergan, Inc. (2016), 247 Cal.App.4th 1, 14-22 [affirming order sustaining demurrer without leave to amend where MDA expressly and impliedly preempted patient's claim that defendant failed to adequately train physicians in use of implantable medical device].) Sapien 3 is a Class III medical device that went through U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) premarket approval and is therefore subject to device-specific federal requirements, including requirements relating to manufacturing and labeling. State common law manufacturing defect claims are expressly preempted to the extent Plaintiff contends that additional or different safety requirements were needed because such allegations challenge FDA's determination that the device was safe for its intended use. (Riegel, 552 U.S. at 321-25; see also Bianco v. Baxter Healthcare Corp. (2008) 158 Cal.App.4th 1039, 1056-57 [MDA preempted state common law causes of action, including strict liability claim for manufacturing defect, in wrongful death action concerning bileaflet mitral heart valve].) Plaintiff may amend her complaint if she can in good faith plead a violation of specific federal regulations or other requirements and explain how California common law manufacturing defect rules parallel, rather than add to, such requirements. (See Riegel, 552 U.S. at 330.) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HOPE OF THE HAIGHT, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. MIKE KAZZOUH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 14, Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing. (Part 1 of 3) Plaintiff Hope of the Haight, Inc.'s application for a preliminary injunction is denied. Plaintiff has failed to establish a likelihood of prevailing on the merits of its claims for fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, and conversion against defendant Mike Kazzouh. The bond previously posted by Mr. Kazzouh is exonerated. Plaintiff is a California corporation that operates a delicatessen and liquor store at 1416 Haight Street in San Francisco. The gist of plaintiff's claims is that Mr. Kazzouh, while working as a cashier at plaintiff's store, converted to his own use a winning lottery ticket with the value of $5 million. Plaintiff claims it was the "rightful custodian" of that ticket and that Mr. Kazzouh has "wrongfully retain[ed] possession of Plaintiff's money in excess of $5,000,000.00." It seeks an injunction preventing Mr. Kazzouh from collecting any monies from the California State Lottery Commission in connection with that lottery ticket, and from assigning or attempting to assign his right to prize payments to a third party. The record is undisputed that Mr. Kazzouh purchased the winning ticket in April 2018 from the market and deli. He submitted the ticket to the California State Lottery Commission for confirmation, and was subject to a routine investigation where it was confirmed that the ticket was genuine, that he had purchased the lottery ticket, and that he was the proper winner of the $5 million prize. The Commission authorized payment of the prize to him, and he elected to receive payment in annual installments in April of each year. He apparently has already received the first such installment payment. Even if it could establish that it would suffer irreparable injury if an injunction is not issued, plaintiff cannot establish any reasonable probability of prevailing on its claims against Mr. Kazzouh. (See Aiuto v. City & County of San Francisco (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 1347, 1361 ("A trial court may not grant a preliminary injunction, regardless of the balance of interim harm, unless there is some possibility that the plaintiff will ultimately prevail on the merits of the claim. [Citation.] 'Where there is . . . no likelihood that the plaintiff will prevail, an injunction favoring the plaintiff serves no valid purpose and can only cause needless harm.'"). First and foremost, plaintiff has not established that it purchased or was the legal owner of the winning ticket, or otherwise has any legitimate interest in the prize. It alleges only that it was the "custodian" of that ticket. However, while a retailer that sells lottery tickets has lawful possession of the tickets until they are sold, plaintiff's mere status as a temporary "custodian" of the ticket does not give it any standing to challenge Mr. Kazzouh's receipt of the prize funds. Put simply, those funds are not "Plaintiff's money." Under controlling California law, a retailer that contracts with the Lottery Commission to sell lottery tickets is not entitled to the proceeds from a winning lottery ticket, even if the ticket is stolen from its inventory by an employee prior to retail sale. Thus, there is little or no likelihood that plaintiff will prevail, and an injunction will not issue. (end part 1 of 3) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HOPE OF THE HAIGHT, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. MIKE KAZZOUH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing (For Tentative Ruling Purposess Only Part 2 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 14, Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 of 3) In Horan v. State of California (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 1503, a California lottery ticket retailer (a delicatessen owner) brought an action against the state, claiming she was entitled to a $100,000 award by reason of her possession of a winning ticket. Her 17-year-old employee had stolen several tickets, scratched them to remove the coating, discovered one was a $100,000 winner, and gave the ticket to his mother, who submitted it to the Commission for redemption. The minor later gave the retailer $40 and a list of the numbers of the tickets he had taken, believing he had thereby "purchased" the tickets from his employer. The retailer reported the theft to the Lottery Commission, which did not make any payment to the minor's mother. After the mother relinquished all rights to the ticket, the retailer filed a complaint against the State of California to collect the proceedings from the winning lottery ticket. The trial court found that plaintiff was not entitled to the prize money. The Court of Appeal agreed and affirmed the judgment, rejecting the plaintiff retailer's contention that she was "the owner of the winning ticket, having purchased it from the Commission by the terms of her retailer contract, and thus has the same entitlement to the prize proceeds as a person would if the ticket had been purchased from a lottery retailer." (Id. at 1508.) The court explained, "Retailers purchase lottery tickets from the Commission at a 5 percent discount. They may either sell the tickets to the public for a profit or play the tickets in their inventory. If any tickets remain unsold at the conclusion of a particular instant game, the retailer may either return the tickets to the Commission for a credit or play them. Since retailers already have title to the lottery tickets in their inventory, they must take an additional step to put them into play." (Id. at 1509.) That step must be consistent with the principle of fair chance, in ignorance of whether the ticket is a winner. (Id.) Because the retailer's employee had already scratched off the ticket, revealing its winning status, plaintiff retailer did not play the ticket consistent with the principle of fair chance, and was not entitled to the prize money. (Id. at 1509-10.) The same result follows here. Even if there were any basis for plaintiff to legitimately claim an ownership interest in the prize money, the Court would decline to issue a preliminary injunction on the ground that the Lottery Commission has primary jurisdiction to determine the ownership interests in question. Under the California State Lottery Act of 1981, Gov't Code §§ 8800-8880.72, the Lottery Commission is required to promulgate regulations to establish a system of verifying the validity of prizes and to effect payment of the prizes. The Act provides that "[n]o prize may be paid arising from tickets . . . that are stolen . . . or not in compliance with additional specific rules and regulations and confidential validation and security tests appropriate to the particular lottery game." (Gov't Code § 8880.321(b).) (end of part 2 of 3) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HOPE OF THE HAIGHT, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. MIKE KAZZOUH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 3 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, January 24, 2019, Line 14, Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction And Temporary Restraining Order Hearing (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 3 Of 3) Under the Commission's regulations, approved April 25, 2013, of which the Court takes judicial notice, prizes may be paid only if all of the following conditions are met: (1) the prize is claimed pursuant to Lottery-established procedures; (2) the claim is submitted within the specified claim period; (3) the original lottery ticket is submitted and determined to be a winning ticket pursuant to the Lottery's validation and authentication procedures; and (4) the claim is submitted by the winner, which the regulations define in relevant part as a player "who legally acquires a winning ticket and owns it at the time it is determined to be a winner." (Regs., §§ 1.0, 5.0, 5.4.2.) To claim an individual winning ticket for a prize of $600 or more, the claimant must complete a claim form and submit it to the Lottery along with the original winning ticket with the information requested on the back of the ticket completed. The Lottery will process the claim and authorize payment only if ticket validation criteria set forth in its regulations have been satisfied; if the Lottery determines that the claim is not valid, it will deny the claim. If the Lottery establishes that a ticket is a winning ticket, but determines that the claimant is not the winner, the Lottery must reject the claim and notify the claimant that the prize must be claimed by and paid to the winner. (Regs., §§ 5.1.3(D), 5.4.7.) The Lottery may investigate a claim if (1) the information on the ticket or on the claim form indicates that the claimant is not the winner, or (2) any other credible evidence indicates that the claimant is not the winner and the director and deputy director of security and law enforcement authorize such investigation. (Regs., § 5.4.6(B).) Under these provisions, if plaintiff had credible evidence that Mr. Kazzouh was not the winner of the ticket in question, it could have presented such evidence to the Commission and requested it to investigate the matter. There is no indication in the record that it ever sought to do so. That the Commission has primary jurisdiction over such matters is an additional factor that weighs heavily against the issuance of the extraordinary injunctive relief sought by plaintiff. (Cf. Jonathan Neil & Assocs., Inc. v. Jones (2004) 33 Cal.4th 917, 931 [doctrine of primary jurisdiction requires resort to an administrative agency to resolve issues within its particular area of expertise].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 3 of 3) =(302/EPS)
